q: The Human Development Report 2025 placed India at which rank? (71st BPSC pattern)
explanation: UNDP's HDR 2025 places India 130th of 193 countries, with HDI 0.685 based on 2023 data. Trap: 131/148 is the Global Gender Gap rank (WEF), 102/123 is GHI, 118/147 was WHR 2025 — all different reports.

q: India's HDI value in HDR 2025 was:
explanation: India's HDI rose from 0.676 (2022 data) to 0.685 (2023 data), remaining in the medium-development category (0.550–0.699). 0.644 was an older value; 0.715 would place India in the high-development group, which it has not yet reached.

q: HDR 2025 reports India's life expectancy at birth (2023) as:
explanation: The UNDP figure is 72.0 years, up from 58.6 in 1990 — the exact fact asked in the 71st BPSC. 70 and 75 are common rounding traps; 67.2 is an older estimate.

q: The Human Development Report is published by:
explanation: UNDP publishes the HDR. UNESCO publishes education reports (e.g., GEM Report); UNICEF focuses on children; the World Bank produces World Development Indicators — a separate database.

q: HDI combines which three broad dimensions?
explanation: HDI measures a long and healthy life (life expectancy), knowledge (expected and mean years of schooling), and a decent standard of living (GNI per capita). It does not capture inflation, gender, or governance directly.

q: India's Global Hunger Index 2025 rank and score were:
explanation: GHI 2025 lists India 102 of 123 with a score of 25.8 (serious). 105/127 is the 2024 edition figure — the number of countries and methodology change yearly, so mixing editions is a trap. 130/193 is HDR; 131/148 is Gender Gap.

q: India's GHI 2025 hunger-severity category was:
explanation: GHI severity bands: low (<5), moderate (5–9.9), serious (10–34.9), alarming (35–49.9), extremely alarming (≥50). India's 25.8 falls squarely in 'serious'. 'Alarming' would need ≥35; 'moderate' needs <10.

q: Which is NOT a component of the Global Hunger Index?
explanation: GHI combines four indicators: undernourishment (share of undernourished population), child wasting, child stunting, and under-five mortality. Maternal mortality is not part of GHI — it belongs to MMR/MMIH reports.

q: The Global Hunger Index 2025 gave India a child wasting figure of:
explanation: India's GHI 2025 components: wasting 18.7%, stunting 32.9%, undernourishment 12.0%, under-five mortality 2.8%. Wasting (low weight-for-height) and stunting (low height-for-age) are often swapped in traps — wasting is the acute, stunting the chronic condition.

q: A correct caution while comparing GHI results across years is:
options: Ranks are directly comparable every year
Only scores are comparable across editions
Rankings and scores are comparable only within the same edition
GHI uses the same country set every year
explanation: GHI's publisher explicitly warns that revised data, methodology changes, and varying country coverage make cross-edition rank/score comparisons invalid. Each edition is self-contained — comparing 102/123 (2025) with 105/127 (2024) is meaningless.

q: The Global Gender Gap Report is released by: (69th BPSC PYQ)
explanation: The World Economic Forum (WEF, Geneva) releases the Global Gender Gap Report — a direct 69th BPSC question. UN Women is a UN entity for gender equality but does not publish this index; UNDP publishes HDR; the World Bank produces separate development databases.

q: India's Global Gender Gap Report 2025 position was:
explanation: WEF ranked India 131st among 148 economies in 2025, with an overall parity score of 64.4%. 118/147 was the 2024 edition (147 countries); the number of economies rose to 148 in 2025. 38/139 is GII; 102/123 is GHI.

q: Match the report to the publisher: HDR, GII, SDG India Index.
explanation: HDR → UNDP; Global Innovation Index → WIPO; SDG India Index → NITI Aayog. WEF publishes the Gender Gap Report (not GII); the World Bank does not publish any of these three; MoSPI publishes PLFS.

q: The Global Innovation Index 2025 placed India at:
explanation: WIPO's GII 2025 ranks India 38th of 139, improving from 39th (2024) and 40th (2023). The upward trajectory is a favourite BPSC angle — 'which is correct about India's GII trend'. 48 is a trap from an older edition.

q: In GII 2025, India ranked first among:
explanation: India was first among lower-middle-income economies and also first in the Central and Southern Asia region. It is not a high-income economy (that group is led by Switzerland, Singapore, the US). 'BRICS only' is a trap — the ranking is global, not BRICS-specific.

q: India's GII 2025 output rank was better than its input rank. The pair (output, input) is:
explanation: India's innovation output rank was 32 (better) and input rank was 52 (weaker) — a rare case where output outperforms input, meaning India extracts more innovation per unit of investment. 38 is the overall GII rank, not a sub-rank.

q: The World Happiness Report 2026 placed India at:
explanation: India ranks 116 in WHR 2026 (score 4.536). WHR 2025 had India at 118 — a 2-rank improvement. Report year matters: mixing 118 (2025) with 116 (2026) is the classic BPSC trap. 131 is the Gender Gap rank.

q: The World Happiness Report is published by:
options: Wellbeing Research Centre (Oxford), Gallup & UN SDSN
explanation: WHR is produced by the Wellbeing Research Centre at the University of Oxford, in partnership with Gallup and the UN Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN). WIPO publishes GII; WEF publishes Gender Gap; UNDP publishes HDR — all separate.

q: India's SDG India Index 2023-24 composite score was: (70th BPSC pattern)
explanation: NITI Aayog reported India's composite score at 71, up from 66 in 2020-21. 57 is Bihar's score (lowest state); 79 is the top state score (Kerala & Uttarakhand). 66 is the previous edition — all four options are real numbers from the same index, making this a classic BPSC trap.

q: Which states jointly topped the SDG India Index 2023-24? (70th BPSC PYQ)
explanation: Kerala and Uttarakhand jointly led with a score of 79. The 70th BPSC asked 'which state topped' with Kerala as the answer; the 2023-24 edition was the first to have a joint topper. Bihar was last at 57.

q: The SDG India Index 2023-24 assesses progress on how many SDGs?
explanation: It assesses 16 SDGs; SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals) is not ranked — it is treated as a common goal across all states. The UN has 17 SDGs total, so '17' is the obvious trap. 15 is an older edition count.

q: The SDG India Index is published by:
explanation: NITI Aayog publishes the SDG India Index — India's own state-level SDG dashboard. UNDP publishes the global HDR; MoSPI publishes PLFS; WEF publishes Gender Gap. All four are distinct publishers with distinct indices.

q: The National Multidimensional Poverty Index in India is published by:
explanation: NITI Aayog publishes India's National MPI, using NFHS data. UNDP co-publishes the global MPI with OPHI (Oxford) — a separate product. RBI focuses on monetary policy; NABARD on rural credit. The national vs global MPI distinction is a BPSC trap.

q: Consider the following statements: 1. GII is published by WIPO. 2. Global Gender Gap Report is published by WEF. 3. HDR is published by UNDP. How many are correct?
explanation: All three publisher-report pairs are correct. These are high-frequency BPSC matching facts: WIPO→GII, WEF→Gender Gap, UNDP→HDR. Students often confuse one of the three, so BPSC bundles them into a statement question.

q: Which pair is WRONGLY matched?
explanation: WIPO publishes GII (Global Innovation Index), not the World Happiness Report. WHR is produced by the Wellbeing Research Centre (Oxford), Gallup & UN SDSN. The other three pairs are all correctly matched.
